original_abstract: We introduce a self-propelled colloidal "docker" made of hematite that can be precision-steered to particle cargo many times its own size. Once activated by visible light, the docker can attach to, transport, and subsequently release the cargo at a desired location. This entire sequence of self-propulsion and docking is fully reversible and light-sensitive. Navigation is achieved either through a weak, uniform magnetic field or by utilizing nanoscale tracks etched into a textured substrate. The underlying mechanism for both motion and docking is rooted in osmotic and phoretic transport within a fuel concentration gradient (hydrogen peroxide), which is sustained by the photocatalytic properties of the hematite. Unlike complex multi-component micro-robots, these hematite dockers are simple, single-component particles that can be synthesized in bulk. This versatile system offers a robust platform for developing micrometer-scale factories and sophisticated biomimetic systems.
humanized_abstract: We present a self-propelled colloidal “docker” made of hematite that can be controlled with precision to pick up, carry and spew out large particle sized loads. Upon exposure to visible light, the docker can bind, transport and release the load at desired locations. The propulsion, picking up and spewing out of the cargo particles is fully reversible and photo triggered. The navigation and movement can be carried out either in a weak uniform magnetic field or along the nanotracks fabricated within the texture of the substrate. The mechanism of both propulsion and cargo docking and releasing is based on an osmotic/phoretic driven flow mediated by fuel (hydrogen peroxide) gradients generated photo catalytically at the hematite surface. Unlike state of the art multi component bio-hybrid robots these single component iron oxide colloidal particles can be fabricated in large quantities. Hence this system presents itself with enormous potential in design of large micrometer sized artificial factories and for the realisation of bio-mimicked functional complex systems.

Hence this system presents itself with enormous potential in design of large micrometer sized artificial factories and for the realisation of bio-mimicked functional complex systems.
